author: @justinw1765
text:
I guess I accidently erased my post earlier when trying to edit it.  Anyways, to reiterate, one of the oldest makers of bamboo bicycles, Calfee, found out the hard way that carbon fiber is not a good choice to wrap/reinforce bamboo joints with because the two materials have very different expansion and contraction rates.  You'll eventually get split bamboo. 

    Hemp, flax linen, fiberglass, basalt, and kevlar (I think?) are better choices than CF.

author: @imaginarymask
text: Carbon fiber is a genius addition!  I am really looking forward to how this turns out!

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: No, it's not.  Bamboo bike makers have found that CF is not a good material to wrap bamboo joints because of the very different expansion and contraction rates of the two materials.  It eventually leads to cracked and/or warped bamboo. Calfee used to use it until they noticed this happening, and then they switched to other reinforcement materials (I think hemp, but fiberglass would be even better.)

replies.author: @imaginarymask
replies.text:
I think the problem is in the expectation that the bamboo wouldn't eventually crack.  Do the tests definitively indicate that it was a problem of expansion and contraction?  My first thought when you mentioned fiberglass was that it might have the same problem, depending on what resin was used.  However, if it was the rigidity of the carbon fiber causing stress to the bamboo, perhaps fiberglass would be more forgiving.  
I searched Calfee, and saw Calfee Designs had made a bamboo bike, which wouldn't really have the same stress in use as this electric 4 wheeler.  I also saw Calfee was a weight loss Youtuber.  Lol
At any rate, my intention was that the idea for carbon fiber was a genius inspiration, the same as what probably inspired the Calfee blokes.  Whether it turns out to be the best option remains to be seen, and my interest is now doubly peaked to see this vehicle completed, and see how the joints do or don't last.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@imaginarymask  Bamboo expands more than CF with both heat and/or moisture. CF is not affected by moisture and expands little with heat. 

   Fiberglass is both less stiff than CF, and has more stretch. Fiberglass fibers when under tension past their tensile strength will stretch some before they break.  CF catastrophically breaks soon after it's ultimate tensile strength has been reached.    In any case, it's more important in this instance that it's like you have a vice overlaid on the bamboo and when the bamboo expands, the CF does not.  The CF being the stronger of the two materials in tensile strength, Young modulus (stiffness strength), etc, it does not "move" with the bamboo.  Hence, the bamboo cracks. 

  You don't need an exact match of expansion/contraction rates, but you need "roughly similar".  Putting it in a specialized epoxy, like G Flex and not wrapping so tight, might also help, but why not go with fiberglass when it's far cheaper than CF, and more sustainable?  And if a person likes natural aesthetics, hemp, linen, and raw bamboo cord will also technically work ok to decently, but in that case you want the strongest (composite type) epoxy you can get to help the natural materials out some as these are far weaker than CF and even fiberglass. 

  (And S-glass fiberglass is not actually far behind common, cheaper commercial forms of CF in strength to weight ratios, though it is less stiff, but is half the price.)

replies.author: @imaginarymask
replies.text: @justinw1765 Makes sense.  Well said.  It would be cool to see some tests done with various materials.  In the pitch video on the Calfee Designs channel, he indicated it would be a good project for students and that he also introduced it in Africa.  I would be interested to see all the data that has been created by all the different iterations done so far, especially from Africa, where it has probably been exposed to a lot of practical use by people that have to make do with what's available.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: @imaginarymask Yes, agreed, it would be nice to see such tests.

author: @reypolice5231
text:
Rob

Soda bottle can be spliced into really long rope easy. Then if you heat it with a hair dryer it shrinks making the joint super tight.

And it free if you find some 2 liter soda bottles. The green ones have built in UV protection. I suppose you'll have to put some UV protection around the shrink wrap soda bottle rope.

But it's free if you make it work.

Power supply is simple, and run of the mill PWM DC controller will work with them.  Just throwing it out there as they are simply amazing motors in my book.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: To adhere to the theme, one can get bamboo cord which is very strong.

replies.author: @reypolice5231
replies.text:
I didn't think of that. That's awesome!!!

It you wet it down with water does the bamboo cord shrink, and get even tighter?

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text: @reypolice5231 Yes, like all cellulose fibres it will change length with the relative humidity (even paper does this). Also, Rob appears to have bought green unseasoned bamboo, which like timber will shrink as it dries. Hence, any lashings will need to be tighten again. The spec sheet should indicate what processes it has undergone like drying, and if any preservative treatment has been applied etc. If it was supplied  for interior decorative use only, it may biodegrade quickly or put another way, this cycle may recycle itself without help from humans.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: @Nuts-Bolts   When buying bamboo cord, make sure it is the raw stuff, and not bamboo rayon.  Bamboo rayon has been through some harsh chemical treatments and is far, far weaker than raw bamboo cord.  Ime so far, it's harder to find raw bamboo cord.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Also keep in mind that bamboo cord, the raw stuff, is also often waxed. This is not good for reinforcement composite type applications because wax has low surface energy and is hard to adhere anything to well.  That's why it's often used as a mold release agent in composites, ironically enough.  You would have to get rid of the wax coating first to use this stuff. Otherwise, your epoxy is likely to peel/shear off of it.
